label: Computational Linguistics
text: Computational linguistics is the field that applies computer science techniques to the study and processing of human language. It involves the development of algorithms and models that allow computers to analyze, generate, and understand natural language. Key applications include machine translation, speech recognition, and text-to-speech systems. Computational linguists also work on parsing, where the structure of sentences is analyzed computationally. This interdisciplinary field is essential for improving human-computer interaction, building AI language models, and processing large-scale linguistic data.

label: Neurolinguistics
text: Neurolinguistics explores the relationship between language and the brain, focusing on how the brain processes and produces language. Researchers in this field study brain activity during language comprehension, production, and development, often using techniques like fMRI and EEG. Neurolinguistics also investigates language disorders, such as aphasia, to better understand how brain damage affects linguistic ability. The goal is to uncover the neural mechanisms that underlie language processing and the ways in which different areas of the brain contribute to various linguistic tasks.

label: Psycholinguistics
text: Psycholinguistics examines the cognitive processes that enable humans to acquire, understand, and produce language. This field bridges psychology and linguistics, focusing on how language is represented in the mind and how language processing occurs in real-time. For example, psycholinguists study how we retrieve words from memory, how we understand ambiguous sentences, and how children learn language. Research methods often include experiments that measure reaction times and brain activity, helping to reveal the mental steps involved in linguistic tasks.

label: Sociolinguistics
text: Sociolinguistics investigates the ways in which language varies across different social groups and contexts. It examines how factors like gender, age, ethnicity, and social class influence language use. Sociolinguists also study language change, dialects, and how people adjust their language in various social settings. For example, a speaker might use more formal language in a job interview than when talking with friends. The field also looks at language ideologies and attitudes, seeking to understand how social structures and cultural norms shape linguistic behavior.

label: Historical Linguistics
text: Historical linguistics focuses on the evolution of languages over time, investigating how languages change in terms of sounds, meanings, and structures. Researchers study language families, tracing the relationships between languages and reconstructing older forms of languages that no longer exist. Key areas include the study of phonological, syntactic, and lexical changes. Historical linguists also examine language contact, borrowing, and the processes of language divergence and convergence. This field provides insight into the development of modern languages and their shared ancestry.
